front: Play: Karl joins outlaws after brother Franz banishes him; Karl stabs his betrothed Amalia to uphold an oath after Roller and Schweizer die
Play: a disguised character presents a sword with 'all-powerful Death dissolves your vow' written in blood
Play: the hero shoots an apple off his son's head after defying a tyrant's command to salute a hat on a pole
Play: John Parricida dresses as a monk to visit the protagonist, who refuses to harbor the murderer; the hero ferries Baumgarten across a stormy lake
Play: Melchtal and a council of three territories postpone revolt until Christmas; coined the aphorism 'the axe in the house spares the carpenter'
Play: trilogy about a Thirty Years' War general assassinated by Gordon and Butler after secret negotiations with the Swedes; consists of Camp, The Piccolomini, and Death
Play: Max Piccolomini intercedes for soldiers; Ottavio betrays the general; speech introduced 'eternal yesterday' into German
Play: Carlos rejects Princess de Eboli for his father's wife Elizabeth of Valois; Marquis of Posa dies for Dutch liberty
Play: begins with jailer Paulet breaking into the protagonist's desk; she confronts her cousin Elizabeth in a park outside her prison
Play: doomed love of Ferdinand von Walter and Luise Miller; President von Walter and secretary Wurm scheme with a fake love letter; both lovers die by poison
work: Intrigue and Love / Kabale und Liebe

front: Work: the 'form' and 'sense' drives combine to create the 'play' drive; responds to Kant's aesthetics; argues art curbs 'lawless instincts'
back: On the Aesthetic Education of Man (Friedrich Schiller)

front: Work: contrasts how modern poets 'touch us through ideas' while ancients 'touch us through nature'; praises Homer's 'dry truthfulness'
back: On Naive and Sentimental Poetry (Friedrich Schiller)

front: Poem: opens 'Vivos voco. Mortuos plango. Fulgura frango'; introduced 'eye of the law' into German
back: The Song of the Bell (Friedrich Schiller)

front: Author: Goethe claimed his skull from a mass grave, deciding the largest must be his; his skull is the focus of a poem in Wilhelm Meister
